summary: Alfred is a deliberately single-threaded, synchronous application (documented in ADR-003). All mutable state is held in a single Rc<RefCell<EditorState>> on the main thread, with no cross-thread sharing. There are no async runtimes, no spawn() calls, and no Arc/Mutex usage in application code. The main concurrency risks are RefCell borrow panics from re-entrant borrows during Lisp hook dispatch (mitigated by the 'deferred action' pattern), the single global AtomicU64 buffer ID counter (correct but uses Relaxed ordering which is sufficient for a single-threaded app), and the unbounded undo_stack which constitutes a memory accumulation risk rather than a race condition. The apply_syntax_highlights function performs a full buffer content read every frame even when the buffer has not changed, which is a minor performance concern.

thread_safety_issues.description: NEXT_BUFFER_ID uses Ordering::Relaxed for fetch_add. In the current single-threaded design this is correct. If threading is ever added, Relaxed is insufficient for unique-ID guarantees across threads; Ordering::Relaxed does not establish happens-before relationships.

race_conditions.description: TOCTOU: `if !init_path.exists()` followed by `fs::read_to_string(&init_path)` -- the file could be deleted between the exists check and the read. In practice this is benign for a local editor but is a classic TOCTOU pattern.
TOCTOU: `if !config_path.exists()` followed by `runtime.eval_file(config_path)` -- config file could be replaced between the check and evaluation. Low risk for a local editor.

n_plus_one.description: apply_syntax_highlights() calls alfred_core::buffer::content(&state.buffer) every render frame to produce a full String clone of the rope, regardless of whether the buffer has changed (no buffer-version check before re-serializing). For large files this allocates a new String on every render tick (100ms poll interval).

memory_leak_risks.description: undo_stack in EditorState is a Vec<UndoSnapshot> with no maximum size. Each snapshot stores a cloned Rope (O(1) clone via structural sharing) plus a Cursor. While individual Rope clones are cheap, after thousands of edits on a large file the accumulated structural sharing tree can grow considerably. The change_list and jump_list both have 100-entry limits but undo_stack has none.
macro_buffer (Vec<KeyEvent>) accumulates all key events during macro recording with no size limit. Pathological recording sessions could grow this buffer arbitrarily.

resource_leaks.description: Buffer::from_file uses std::fs::read_to_string which opens and closes the file handle atomically. No manual handle management -- this is safe. Noted for completeness only.

recommendations.title: Cap undo_stack at a configurable maximum (e.g., 1000 entries)
recommendations.description: Add an UNDO_MAX_SIZE constant to push_undo() and drain the oldest entries when the limit is exceeded. This mirrors the existing pattern used for jump_list and change_list. The existing Rope structural sharing makes individual snapshot cost minimal, but accumulated tree nodes across thousands of snapshots can still be non-trivial.

recommendations.title: Skip full buffer serialization in apply_syntax_highlights when buffer version is unchanged
recommendations.description: Store the last-highlighted buffer version in SyntaxHighlighter. In apply_syntax_highlights, compare state.buffer.version() against the stored version before calling buffer::content(). This avoids an O(n) string allocation every 100ms render tick when the user is not typing.

recommendations.title: Upgrade NEXT_BUFFER_ID to Ordering::SeqCst if threading is ever introduced
recommendations.description: The current Relaxed ordering for the buffer ID counter is correct in the single-threaded model. Add a comment documenting this assumption, and upgrade to SeqCst if worker threads are ever added for background syntax parsing.

recommendations.title: Add a size limit to macro_buffer
recommendations.description: Cap macro recording at a reasonable size (e.g., 10,000 key events) to prevent pathological memory consumption from very long recording sessions.
